title: Expressing support for the designation of May 17, 2025, as "Necrotizing Enterocolitis Awareness Day".
plain_summary: This bill asks Congress to support making May 17, 2025 a special day to teach people about necrotizing enterocolitis. This is a very serious disease that hurts the intestines of premature babies who are born too early. It is the main reason why premature babies die in hospitals. The disease affects thousands of babies every year and costs billions of dollars to treat. Black babies get this disease more often than white babies.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for making May seventeenth, two thousand twenty-five a day to raise awareness about necrotizing enterocolitis.

sections.title: Whereas Clauses
sections.text: Necrotizing enterocolitis (a serious disease that damages the intestines) is the most common cause of death in hospitalized premature babies after they are two weeks old. Babies born early or with medical problems like heart defects are most likely to get this disease. Thousands of babies develop necrotizing enterocolitis each year and hundreds of babies die from this complicated condition. This disease causes severe swelling that can damage intestinal tissue and cause death. Once doctors diagnose necrotizing enterocolitis, many babies only live for a few hours or days. Surgery for this disease does not cure it, and babies who survive often have lifelong problems with their brain development and nutrition. This disease costs about five billion dollars in hospital expenses each year and makes up roughly nineteen percent of all newborn healthcare spending in the United States. Each case that requires surgery costs an estimated two hundred thousand dollars or more. Breast milk is one of the best ways to help prevent this disease in medically fragile babies. When the mother's milk is not available, pasteurized donated human milk is the next safest option for babies at risk. Formula does not protect against the risk of this disease in medically fragile babies. Babies at risk get the most protection from their mother's own milk and pasteurized donated milk. Although the risk of this disease cannot be completely eliminated and there are not yet sure ways to stop or cure it, preventative measures work well at reducing risks. Parents must be trusted and valued as the most important member of their baby's care team. Healthcare providers should work with families to provide the best care for babies at risk. This disease can develop in both premature babies and babies born at full term. More research data is desperately needed to better understand how and why this disease happens in both early and full-term babies. Differences and inconsistencies in care lead to Black babies being more likely to be affected by and die from this disease compared to White babies. To build a world without this disease and improve outcomes for babies and families, Americans must come together to raise awareness about this devastating newborn disease.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes the importance of raising awareness about necrotizing enterocolitis and expresses support for designating May seventeenth, two thousand twenty-five as Necrotizing Enterocolitis Awareness Day.
